sentences_input.text: Yes, this is my daughter, Sandra.
She's married to Arnold Wagner, a lovely husband she has.
She has four children-- two girl-- three girls and one boy.
The oldest one is Rachel, Avital, Micky, and the young boy is David.

There-- there is an enormous amount one could say that covers all the aspects of having been brought up with a survivor and the traumas and the nightmares and the getting-- the times that were easier, and the slowly, slowly, the way stories came out, bit by bit-- the inability to talk and then the release.
And it's very interesting that mummy felt she could talk to my husband and to my sister's husband more easily than she ever felt she could talk to my sister and I.

She talks to the children a little bit, but then some of them are still very young and she's very sensitive to what she thinks they can cope with and she doesn't want to traumatize them, frighten them.
She only wants to give them what she feels they are able to handle.
The big one she tells more.
She's very close to Rachel.
She's-- she's the one that she has always spoken French to.

She looked after her quite regularly when she was tiny, in a way that she never had to with any of the others, although she has them very often and they're very-- they're mutually very fond of each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's a responsibility that go-- that goes with being the daughter of a survivor in that one feels one is replacing a lost generation, or so many that were lost.
And one feels that one wants to carry on.
And the idea of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is always with us in a lot  of what we do and how we act and how we conduct our lives.
I think that's what I wanted to say.
Yes.
I didn't mention [INAUDIBLE], which I witnessed with several of the kommando What happened was we were-- we were from that day onwards when we-- we were caught at the kommando [INAUDIBLE].
We were from then on only in [? schaff ?] kommandos.
And that time, we were working very near the crematoriums.
I thought there was an eclipse.
It-- it was dark.
It was black.
And yet, it was in the afternoon, because we knew we finished at dark.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suddenly, we saw people being dragged, like all strung up, one after the other.
And they didn't notice us yet, that we were still working, and they said to us that we have to right away get out of here and go back to our bags.

What we heard afterwards, because there was a big appeal later, was that this was a group of people, amongst them was Mala, and some men who had tried to get-- tried to get-- get out of Auschwitz that was very well organized, but were caught the last minute and were brought back.
It was-- they were going to tell the world what was going on in October, '44. '45-- '44.
It was October '44.
And we felt terrible that these people were caught.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing we heard was that there was a big appeal, a call up.
The whole camp was out and these people were going to be executed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also heard that Mala was amongst them, and we understood that she tried-- I can only repeat from what I heard myself in the camp.
I don't know if this was actually a fact-- that she tried to cut her wrists before she was being hung.
And eventually, she wasn't even dead, they told us.
That's what they told us in the camp.
And she was thrown into the-- into the crematorium.
And she had a horrible end.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we actually saw her being brought back at the time.
We happened to work exactly there between the crematorium and a, a, a fence where they were being-- behind them they were being brought back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I suppose there are different stories about what happened to them, but that's what we saw and what we were told immediately after.
Yes, I-- I'm coming back to Bergen-Belsen now.
We stayed on for another few weeks. 'Till February we stayed on, with the same terrible conditions.
And by then, we were also full of lice because I can't remember the delousing, but I remember that we tried to wash ourselves in ice cold water outside and we tried to keep ourselves clean.
But we were covered with [INAUDIBLE] from no proper food, no proper hygiene.
And we were full of boils.
